title: Lesson 408
transcript: Okay, let's now move up to talking about how we can suffer the loss of an entire data center and still be able to recover from that. And that's where we're now gonna talk about different recovery site strategies. What these are all about is what do you do if your entire data center fails? How are you gonna recover from that? And we're going to talk through a bunch of different options here. And the basic difference between all of them is as you're willing to pay more money, as the costs go up, the recovery time goes down, right? So if you're only willing to pay a very tiny bit of money for like, say a cold site, your recovery time is probably going to be weeks. And as you pay more and more and more money, moving up to all the way up to say like a redundant or mere site, your recovery time goes down to seconds, but your costs go way up. So that's the fundamental difference between all of these. And I'm going to explain them all in detail here. But before I get into explaining the difference between a cold site, a hot site, a warm site, et cetera, let me first define these different ways that we're going to evaluate. These are sort of the different capabilities that these recovery sites provide. So let's start with recovery time. Recovery time is the approximate amount of time that it's going to take to recover a certain type of site and put a star in your workbook beside this one, pay attention to this one. You should remember these recovery times. This is a really common exam question might ask you approximately how long does it take to recover a cold site or a warm site? Remember these approximate recovery times. Cost is just relative cost, not very important. Okay, now here's what infrastructure and HVAC means. Infrastructure HVAC basically means just the empty shell of a building. So imagine an empty warehouse with nothing in it. And the only thing you have is some power coming into the building, some utilities, power, network, things like that. And you probably have an HVAC unit up on the roof. But otherwise, it is a completely empty warehouse. That's what infrastructure HVAC means, an empty warehouse with nothing in it. Basic equipment is the cheap equipment. Cheap equipment is things like the actual server racks, not the servers themselves, just the racks that they go in and the power cables and the network cables and things like that. So basic equipment is the cheap equipment. You've put all the racks in place, you've run power cables to the racks, network cables to the racks, et cetera, but you're still missing the expensive equipment, which is the computer hardware. So computer hardware is exactly what it sounds like. It's the expensive equipment. It's the actual physical servers, firewalls, things like that. Computer hardware is the expensive equipment. And data is data and people are people. Probably don't need to define those ones. Okay, now, given those definitions, let's now look at what the different recovery sites provide. And we'll start with a cold site. All a cold site provides is an empty warehouse with nothing in it. Just the basic infrastructure and HVAC. So imagine you need to recover an entire data center and you're starting with just an empty warehouse with absolutely nothing in it. How long is it going to take you to get a data center up and running in there? Weeks. It's going to take you weeks. So remember that. The approximate recovery time for a cold site. Because all it provides is an empty building with nothing in it. The recovery time is weeks. Okay, let's now move up to a warm site. A warm site is the basic shell of a building and the cheap equipment. You've got all the server racks sitting there, you've run all the power cables, et cetera. And the way that most organizations will set this up with a warm site is they'll essentially have a whole shopping list of all the expensive equipment they need sitting almost like in a shopping cart, just ready for them to hit the buy now button. And if an emergency ever does occur and they need to get their warm site up and running, then they'll hit the buy now button. There's actually companies, suppliers that specialize in this. They maintain an inventory of systems. And if a company ever has an emergency and they need to get their warm site up and running, the company will have sort of basically a shopping list all ready to go of like, here we go, here's all the equipment we need. The supplier will overnight send all that equipment, you know, overnight shipping via FedEx or UPS or something to the customer. So yeah, this is gonna cost a lot of money, but what's gonna happen is right, disaster occurs, The company says, ah, we're going to activate our warm site. So they put in a call to their supplier. The supplier overnight ships all the equipment they need. So it's all there the next morning. All the racks and power cables are already all set up. So all the recovery team has to do is basically unbox all the servers, stick them into the racks to connect them to power and network. recover the data and you're back up and running in a matter of days. That's a warm site. Let's now move all the way up to a hot site. And a hot site means that you have all of the equipment. You've got the empty shell of a warehouse. You've got all the basic equipment and you've got all the expensive equipment. So remember that a hot site means that all of the equipment that you need is sitting there ready to go. A hot site provides all the equipment. But this is where we have to kind of, there's actually various different ways that hot sites can be run. And there's huge cost implications involved in this and differences in recovery times. And that's why it's anywhere between seconds and hours. That's a big difference, right? A few seconds to a few hours. Why the difference? Well, it's about whether you're actually staffing and running your hot site 24 by 7 or not. So the cheaper type of hot site is where the hot site has all the equipment sitting there ready to go, but it's all powered down. All the equipment is just sitting there turned off. None of the lights are on. There's no people there. you know, backups are being stored there. And if a disaster ever does occur, the recovery team is gonna have to rush out to the site, power everything on, recover all the data to the systems, and you're gonna be back up and running in a matter of hours in that case. The other extreme of a hotsite is this is a fully operational data center, staffed, powered on, running 24 by 7. So in this option, in this option of the hotsite where it's staffed running 24 by 7, What you're probably doing is some sort of real time replication from a primary site to your hot site of critical data. And so if your primary site ever fails, you can get your hot site up and running in a matter of seconds because it's a fully operational data center staff 24 by seven. You're doing real time data replication. And so if the primary fails, you can get your house site up and running in a matter of seconds. So that's why there's this variability from a few seconds to a few hours. It all depends on whether your hot site is being staffed, run 24 by 7 or not. And that's obviously a huge cost implication from people's time, electricity, etc. Okay, now just to go a little deeper into hot sites, there's actually two different types of hot sites that you need to know about. The first we'll talk about here is a mobile site. A mobile site is a hot site on wheels. Typically what organizations do here is they'll get a shipping container and then they'll stuff a whole bunch of servers inside the shipping container. So the shipping container is the data center basically. And then what makes it a mobile site, of course, is that you can take that shipping container and you can put it on the back of a truck and you can drive it to wherever you need it. A perfect example of this, in the US, there's an organization called FEMA, which I don't remember what it stands for. You can Google it. FEMA is the federal agency that responds to natural disasters. So when Florida or Texas get hit with a massive tornado and there's a bunch of recovery actions that have to occur, There's a bunch of coordination that has to happen, and FEMA will handle that coordination. And the way they'll do that is they have mobile hot sites. They have hot sites on wheels that they can drive to wherever there's a disaster and coordinate the disaster. So that's an example of a mobile hot site, basically just a hot site on wheels. And then, this is the most expensive option. A mere site or a redundant site. That's the type of hot site that I was talking about that's staffed and operating... 24 by seven. A mirror site is specifically what a mirror site is. It's the type of hot site that you're operating staffing 24 by seven. And so a mirror site has all, you know, it's not only warehouse, not only basic equipment, not only all the hardware. So you've got all the hardware you need. you're doing real time data replication and you're staffing this mirror site, this hot site 24 by seven. And so your recovery time is seconds, maybe even near instantaneously for some really critical applications if you're doing like absolute real time data replication. Out of all these options here, the Mir site is obviously by far the most expensive. It's the type of hot site that's staffed, running, operating 24 by 7. Very expensive option. But there's actually one more even expensive option. It's known as multiple processing sites. And I'll talk about them in just a minute here. But there you go. That's the basic differences that you should remember between cold, warm, and hot sites. Remember the approximate recovery times. And just remember mobile and mirror sites are two different examples of hot sites. and I'll get to what multiple processing sites are in just a second. Here's this term that I've introduced earlier and I'll now talk about in a little more detail, geographically remote or geographical disparity. What this is, geographical disparity, it's not any set distance. It's not 10 kilometers or 50 miles or whatever. What it means is that it's far enough away that whatever disaster befalls the primary site isn't going to take out your recovery site or your backups or whatever. So geographically remote or geographical disparity simply means that it's far enough away that whatever disaster, flood, fire, earthquake, power outage, war, whatever, whatever took out your primary site, isn't going to take out your recovery site. And so that's why it's not any set distance. So for instance, I live on Vancouver Island, just over here. Vancouver Island is massive. You can drive many, many, many, many hours north. A long, long way. But what would not be geographically remote would be to have a data center on the southern tip of the island where I am and then a data center on like the northern tip of the island. Like, yeah, that's a huge distance apart. But both data centers would be on the same island. And this is a very geographically active area. At some point, there's going to be a massive earthquake. The whole island is going to sink into the Pacific. Or there's going to be a massive tsunami. And that massive tsunami, massive earthquake is going to take out everything on the island. geographically remote would not be anywhere else on Vancouver Island. It would be on the other side of the Coast Mountains or on the other side of the Rocky Mountains. That would be geographically remote for where I am. So that's why the distance varies. So if you had a data center here, geographically remote might be on the other side of the Rocky Mountains or maybe over on the East Coast or Europe or something like that. That would be geographically remote. And that now leads us to talking about multiple processing sites. Multiple processing sites is where you have multiple geographically remote data centers that are all working together to support a workload. This is even more expensive than mirror sites. Because in mirror sites, you have a primary and then you have your recovery mirror site. In multiple processing sites, you have two, usually three geographically remote data centers that are all working together. why would you wanna do this? Because this is really expensive. Well, it's if the risk of downtime or outage was super high and the cost would be astronomical. So the perfect example of this, and this is organizations that do this, is credit card processing companies. Like imagine, how much would it cost Visa if their credit card processing capability went down on Black Friday. Honestly, I don't know what the numbers are, but it would be a pretty astronomical number, right? It would cost Visa many, many, many millions of dollars per second, right? If their credit card processing capability went down on, say, Black Friday. And so this is where they run multiple processing sites. And the way this typically works, and this is how the credit card companies do it. When you go to pay for something with your credit card, that transaction actually goes through two, usually three different data centers at the exact same time, multiple processing. So the same transaction will go through, say, all three data centers. And all three of those data centers are geographically remote from each other. And the reason that the credit card companies will usually use three data centers is there's another benefit you can get here, and that's data integrity. So if you run the same transaction through three data centers, you can compare the results. And if two data centers get one answer and another one gets a different one, something you want to look at. And of course, if one of those data centers goes down, zero impact, right? Because the same transaction is going through one or maybe even two other data centers. If you had three multiple processing sites, you could lose two entire data centers and you'd still be up and running. So extremely expensive solution. But... it might be cost justified. And for credit card companies, it is. A few more little things that we have to talk about here related to recovery sites. You may come across this terminology on the exam, internal versus external. So you might see an internal hot site or an external hot site or an internal warm site or an external warm site. All this is getting at is who owns the recovery site. So if it's Acme Corporation and they own their own cold site, that would be considered to be an internal cold site. Internal means it's owned and operated by the organization. There's also company service providers that provide recovery sites as a service. Perfect example of one of these companies is called SunGuard. So you can pay SunGuard a certain amount of money every month and they will maintain cold sites or warm sites available such that you know if you ever have a disaster they'll say okay yeah no problem we've got this building over here you can go and you recover your systems there so that would be an external cold site or an external warm site because it's provided by a service provider Another thing related to these recovery sites is what are known as reciprocal agreements. Reciprocal agreements is where essentially two organizations agree that, okay, if one of us goes down, so let's say we've got organizations A and B here, Organization A would say, OK, well, if our data center ever goes down, organization B will say, yeah, no problem. We've got some spare capacity in our data center, so come over here and you can recover your systems in our data center, and vise versa. If B goes down, Company A will say, yeah, no problem, right? We've got some spare capacity over in our data center here. You can come and recover your systems in our data center. So that would be a reciprocal agreement. Two organizations agreeing that if either one has an outage, the other can recover their systems. Reciprocal agreements are extremely rare in the commercial space. Like did you know that Coke and Pepsi have a reciprocal agreement? Of course not, right? They hate each other. It would never happen. So reciprocal agreements really don't happen in the commercial space. Where you much more commonly see reciprocal agreements is in, say, government agencies. So for example, where I live in British Columbia, the Ministry of Education has a reciprocal agreement with the Ministry of Finance, right? These are two government ministries that are both there to serve the people. They have two different data centers. And so if the Ministry of Education ever goes down, the Ministry of Finance says, yeah, you know, you can come and recover some of your systems in our data center or vice versa. that's where you'll tend to see reciprocal agreements is in between say government agencies. Another little bit of information here related to these different recovery sites, resource capacity agreements. Like let's say you have a cold site or a warm site or even a hot site. You wanna make sure ahead of time that your network service provider is gonna be able to provide enough network bandwidth. or the power company is going to be able to provide enough power to actually power up your data center. Or if you've got a fuel company that's going to be coming on site to provide fuel to your recovery site for the backup generators, you want to make sure that they can provide enough fuel in a timely manner. So this is where resource capacity agreements come in. pre-arranged agreements with vendors to ensure that whatever resources you need in the event of a disaster, that service provider, that vendor is going to be able to provide sufficient resources. So that's resource capacity agreements. All right, so here's that question again of which of these is best, which is better, like a cold site or a warm site or a redundant site or a multiple processing sites. And the answer is, as I said before, It depends. There is no best here. It all depends on things like your recovery point objective, how much data the organization and the owner is willing to lose as a measure of time, And the RTO, how quickly do you need to be able to recover things? So if the owner says, you know what? I'm fine with it taking a few weeks to get our data center back up and running. go ahead, use the cheapest option, use a cold site. Whereas if the owner says, I can only, I can't have my data center down more than two seconds, you're gonna have to pay for a way more expensive option, like say a redundant site. Or if the owner says, I'm only willing to lose two seconds worth of data, you probably have to use some sort of like mere backup solution or clustering or redundancy. So none of these is best. It depends on whatever best fits the needs of the organization. What's the RPO? What's the RTO? How much is the organization willing to pay? Final little thing I'll cover here is if you ever come across these terms like resiliency, high availability, quality of service, fault tolerance, how do we achieve all these things? Through all the techniques that we've just talked about through hot spares, through RAID 6 arrays, through redundant databases or clustering or multiple processing sites or whatever. So the way that you achieve these things, high availability, quality of service, fault tolerance, et cetera, is using all the different recovery techniques that we've just spent the last good chunk of time talking through here.
